covid lungs: The image displays bilateral, peripheral ground-glass opacities (GGOs), a hallmark finding in COVID-19 pneumonia.
A 'crazy-paving' pattern is visible, characterized by ground-glass opacities with superimposed interlobular septal thickening.
The scan shows multifocal opacities with a posterior and lower-lobe predominance, consistent with COVID-19.
Consolidative opacities are seen in the lung periphery, often with an associated air bronchogram.
The image reveals rounded or nodular ground-glass opacities, a common morphological presentation of COVID-19.
A 'reverse halo' sign or atoll sign is present, which appears as a central ground-glass opacity surrounded by a denser ring of consolidation.
The image demonstrates vascular thickening and enlargement within the areas of lung opacity.
In later stages, fibrotic changes such as traction bronchiectasis and architectural distortion are visible.
The scan is notable for the relative absence of pleural effusion, which is uncommon in early COVID-19.
The distribution of opacities is subpleural and bilateral, sparing the immediate tissue around the bronchi.
lung opacity lungs: The image shows a focal lung opacity, appearing as a localized area of increased radiodensity within the lung parenchyma.
A diffuse opacity is visible, characterized by widespread, poorly defined increased attenuation throughout the lungs.
The chest radiograph reveals a well-defined nodular opacity, suggestive of a solitary pulmonary nodule.
The scan demonstrates an area of consolidation, which is an opacity that obscures the underlying bronchial and vascular markings.
A linear opacity is present, which could be consistent with atelectasis, scarring, or a fibrotic band.
The image displays a reticular opacity, composed of a network of fine lines, indicating interstitial lung disease.
A cavitary opacity is visible, showing a lucent center within a consolidative mass, suggesting necrosis.
The opacity has an 'air bronchogram' sign, where air-filled bronchi are visible against the opaque lung.
This photo shows a patchy opacity, indicating an airspace disease process with incomplete filling of the alveoli.
The image presents a ground-glass opacity (GGO), a hazy increase in lung density that does not obscure the underlying vessels.
normal lungs: The image shows clear and well-aerated lung fields, with no evidence of focal or diffuse opacities.
Normal pulmonary vascular markings are visible, branching appropriately from the hila towards the lung periphery.
The chest radiograph displays sharp and distinct costophrenic angles and hemidiaphragmatic contours.
In this scan, the lung parenchyma is free of any nodules, masses, or areas of abnormal consolidation.
The trachea and main bronchi are centrally located and appear patent, consistent with a normal study.
The cardiac silhouette and mediastinal contours are of normal size and shape for the patient's age and build.
No signs of pleural effusion or pneumothorax are visible in the pleural spaces.
The visualized bony structures, such as the ribs and clavicles, appear intact and without lesions.
Both hila are of normal size and density, without evidence of adenopathy or mass.
The image demonstrates a normal lung volume with no signs of hyperinflation or restrictive patterns.
viral pneumonia lungs: The image demonstrates bilateral, diffuse interstitial opacities, a common radiographic pattern in viral pneumonia.
A diffuse reticular or reticulonodular pattern is visible throughout the lung fields, suggesting interstitial inflammation.
The scan shows widespread ground-glass opacities, consistent with acute viral pneumonitis.
Peribronchial cuffing, seen as thickening and indistinctness of the airway walls, is evident in this image.
Unlike typical bacterial pneumonia, this scan lacks focal lobar consolidation, instead showing diffuse, patchy involvement.
The image reveals small, ill-defined centrilobular nodules scattered throughout both lungs.
There is evidence of interstitial edema, characterized by fine septal lines (Kerley B lines) in the lung periphery.
The overall lung volumes appear normal or slightly hyperinflated, which can be a feature of viral infection.
The scan shows a 'tree-in-bud' pattern in some areas, indicating inflammation and fluid in the small airways.
Pleural effusions, if present, are typically small and bilateral, unlike the larger effusions sometimes seen in bacterial pneumonia.
